content:
You are the Accumulator. Given one paper (markdown + optionally a cloned
GitHub repo), do TWO things:
  1. Assign the paper to ONE of the 7 ML domains below.
  2. Extract 1–3 distinct main contributions as seeds.

Each seed captures (a) the underlying problem the paper addresses, and
(b) the method that solves it, including what is special / novel about
the approach. Avoid splitting one idea into multiple sub-seeds; prefer
fewer, higher-quality seeds.

## Domains (pick exactly one)
- LLM_NLP    : language models, NLP, text-only agents
- MULTIMODAL : cross-modality (text+image, text+audio, etc.).
               Vision-Language Models (VLM, e.g., CLIP / LLaVA / GPT-4V)
               belong HERE, not under CV.
- CV         : visual perception (classification, detection, segmentation,
               video understanding) AND image/video generation
               (diffusion, GAN, VAE) when the contribution is the
               generative mechanism on visual data.
- RL         : reinforcement learning algorithms, policies, environments;
               RLHF when the contribution is the RL algorithm itself.
- IR_REC     : information retrieval, recommender systems, search.
- SPEECH     : speech / audio (TTS, ASR, audio language models).
- ROBOTICS   : embodied agents, manipulation, planning in physical world.
               If a paper proposes an embodied policy, prefer ROBOTICS
               over RL.

## Workspace contract
Paper directory:    /work/1912.01321
paper.md            Pre-converted markdown of the PDF.
repo/               Will exist if you `git clone` it. Otherwise unavailable.

## Tools
- Read       : view paper.md and any file under the paper directory
- Grep       : search inside paper.md or the cloned repo
- Bash       : `git clone <url> /work/1912.01321/repo` (only if you've found a
               *clearly official* GitHub URL in the paper);
               also general filesystem exploration: tree / ls / find / wc

## Output (terminate the run)
When you have finished, stop calling tools and reply with a SINGLE JSON
object inside a ```json fenced block. No extra prose:
  ```json
  {"title": "...", "domain": "<one of 7>", "seeds": [{"problem": "...", "method": "..."}, ...]}
  ```
- title  = the paper's actual title (NOT a venue label or page header)
- domain = exactly one of LLM_NLP / MULTIMODAL / CV / RL / IR_REC / SPEECH / ROBOTICS
- seeds  = 1–3 entries, or empty list if extraction failed

## Default flow
1. Read paper.md end-to-end. Identify the paper's true title (often the
   first H1, but PDF→md sometimes places venue labels / preprint headers
   above it — pick the actual title, not the noise). For longer papers
   a single Read may not cover everything; continue with Read(offset=...)
   until you actually understand the methodology and experiments
   sections, not just the abstract and introduction.
2. Decide the domain from the 7 above. Use the tie-breakers in the list
   when a paper sits between two.
3. Look for an official GitHub URL (abstract, footnote 1, "Code:" line, etc.).
   If you find one and it is unambiguously the paper's repo, clone it.
   If you are unsure, do NOT clone — proceed paper-only.
4. (If repo cloned) Use Bash / Grep to locate where the main contribution
   is implemented. Read enough to confirm what's special.
5. Draft 1–3 candidate seeds in your reasoning. Each seed:
     - problem: 1–2 sentences. The challenge the paper addresses.
     - method: 3–5 sentences. The solution AND what is special about it.
6. BEFORE emitting the final JSON, verify each candidate seed against
   the paper. For each seed:
     - Identify 2-3 specific claims your `method` text makes
       (mechanism name, key design choice, quantitative finding).
     - Use Grep on paper.md to locate where each claim is supported,
       and Read(offset=...) to inspect the surrounding context if the
       Grep snippet is insufficient. (Use repo Grep/Read if cloned.)
     - Revise the seed text to match the paper. If a claim cannot be
       grounded after re-reading, REMOVE that claim or drop the seed
       entirely.
   This step is MANDATORY: do not skip it because a seed feels right.
7. Stop calling tools and emit the final JSON object described under
   "## Output". This terminates the run.

Budget: 100 tool calls. Plan first, then act.

## Paper
Paper directory: /work/1912.01321

Begin by reading paper.md.

name: Read
content:
1	## Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function
     2	
     3	Zifeng Wang 1 , Hong Zhu 2 * , Zhenhua Dong 2 † , Xiuqiang He 2 , Shao-Lun Huang 1
     4	
     5	1 Tsinghua-Berkeley Shenzhen Institute, Tsinghua University 2 Noah's Ark Lab, Huawei
     6	
     7	Email: wangzf18@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn, zhuhong8@huawei.com, dongzhenhua@huawei.com, hexiuqiang1@huawei.com, shaolun.huang@sz.tsinghua.edu.cn
     8	
     9	## Abstract
    10	
    11	In the time of Big Data , training complex models on largescale data sets is challenging, making it appealing to reduce data volume for saving computation resources by subsampling. Most previous works in subsampling are weighted methods designed to help the performance of subset-model approach the full-set-model, hence the weighted methods have no chance to acquire a subset-model that is better than the full-set-model. However, we question that how can we achieve better model with less data? In this work, we propose a novel Unweighted Influence Data Subsampling (UIDS) method, and prove that the subset-model acquired through our method can outperform the full-set-model. Besides, we show that overly confident on a given test set for sampling is common in Influence-based subsampling methods, which can eventually cause our subset-model's failure in out-ofsample test. To mitigate it, we develop a probabilistic sampling scheme to control the worst-case risk over all distributions close to the empirical distribution. The experiment results demonstrate our methods superiority over existed subsampling methods in diverse tasks, such as text classification, image classification, click-through prediction, etc.
    12	
    13	## Introduction
    14	
    15	Bigger data can probably train a better model. It is almost the common sense nowadays for machine learning and deep learning practitioners. In the classical Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) theory, it assumes that our training samples and test samples are i.i.d drawn from the same distribution. The learning theory tries to minimize the estimate of the generalization risk, namely the empirical risk. Therefore, when training samples are large enough, we can have hypothesis function working well on the test set by optimizing on the empirical risk.
    16	
    17	However, the ERM faces several challenges: 1) a model is learned from training set which is generated by P ( x, y ) = p train ( x ) p ( y | x ) , and the model is tested on Q ( x, y ) = q test ( x ) p ( y | x ) . The distribution shift from P to Q violates the ERM's basic assumption; 2) unknown noise in data and its label is common in reality, causing some examples harmful for model's performance (Szegedy et al. 2014; Zhang et al. 2019); 3) training on large data sets imposes significant burden on computation, some large-scale deep learning models require hundreds even thousands of GPUs.
    18	
    19	* This author contributes equally with Zifeng Wang.
    20	
    21	† Corresponding author.
    22	
    23	Copyright © 2020, Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (www.aaai.org). All rights reserved.
    24	
    25	Specifically, the subsampling approaches are initially proposed to cope with the last challenge. By virtue of subtle sampling regime, the selected subset can best approximate the original full set in terms of data distribution, hence the model can be trained on a compressed version of data set. In our work, we attempt to design sampling regime that can not only reduce the computation complexity, but also deal with several other ERM's difficulties. For instance, reweighting examples by sampling probabilities to fix mismatch between P and Q , and dropping noisy samples to strengthen the model's generalization ability.
    26	
    27	Our work can be outlined in four points. First, instead of approaching the full-set-model ˆ θ , we prove that the model ˜ θ trained by a selected subset through our subsampling method can outperform the ˆ θ ; second, we propose several probabilistic sampling functions, and analyze how the sampling function influences the worst-case risk (Bagnell 2005) changes over a χ 2 -divergence ball. We further propose a surrogate metric to measure the confidence degree of the sampling methods over the observed distribution, which is useful for evaluating model's generalization ability on a set of distributions; third, for the sake of implementation efficiency, the Hessian-free mixed Preconditioned Conjugate Gradient (PCG) method is used to compute the influence function (IF) in sparse scenarios; last, complete experiments are conducted on diverse tasks to demonstrate our methods superiority over the existing state-of-the-art subsampling methods 1 .
    28	
    29	## Related work
    30	
    31	There are two main ideas to cope with the ERM challenges aforementioned: 1) pessimistic method that tries to learn model robust to noise or bad examples, including l 2 -norm regularization, Adaboost (Freund and Schapire 1997), hard example mining (Malisiewicz, Gupta, and Efros 2011) and focal loss (Lin et al. 2017); and 2) optimistic method that modifies the input distribution directly. There are several genres of optimistic methods: the example reweighting method is used for dealing with distribution shift by (Bagnell 2005; Hu et al. 2016), and handling data bias by (Kumar, Packer, and Koller 2010; Ren et al. 2018); the sample selection method is applied to inspect and fix mislabeled data by (Zhang, Zhu, and Wright 2018). However, few of them have worked on alleviating the computational burden in terms of big data.
    32	
    33	1 The code can be found at https://github.com/RyanWangZf/ Influence Subsampling
    34	
    35	In order to reduce computation, the weighted subsampling method has been explored to approximate the maximum likelihood with a subset on the logistic regression (Fithian and Hastie 2014; Wang, Zhu, and Ma 2018), and on the generalized linear models (Ai et al. 2018). (Ting and Brochu 2018) introduces the IF in weighted subsampling for asymptotically optimal sampling probabilities for several generalized linear models. However, it is still an open problem about how to treat high variance of weight terms for weighted subsampling.
    36	
    37	Specifically, the IF is defined by the Gateaux derivatives within the scope of Robust Statistics (Huber 2011), and extended to measure example-wise influence (Koh and Liang 2017) and feature-wise influence (Sliwinski, Strobel, and Zick 2019) on validation loss. The family of IF is mainly applied to design adversarial example and explain behaviour of black-box model previously. Recently, the IF on validation loss is used for targeting important samples, (Wang, Huan, and Li 2018) builds a sample selection scheme on Deep Convolutional Networks (CNN), and (Sharchilev et al. 2018) builds specific influential samples selection algorithm for Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT). However, by far there has no systematic theorem to guide IF's use in subsampling. Our work tries to build theoretical guidance for IFbased subsampling, which combines reweighting and subsampling together to synthetically cope with ERM's challenges, e.g. distribution shift and noisy data.
    38	
    39	## Preliminaries
    40	
    41	glyph[negationslash]
    42	
    43	Training samples z 1 , z 2 , .., z n ∈ X × Y are generated from P ( x, y ) , and x ∈ R d where the d is the number of feature dimension. Specifically for classification task, we have hypothesis function h θ ∈ H : R d → R parameterized by θ ∈ Θ . The goal is to minimize the 0-1 risk R θ ( P ) = E P [ I ( h θ ( x ) = y )] , and learn the optimal ˆ θ . For computational tractability, researchers focus on minimization of the surrogate loss , e.g. the log loss for binary classification:
    44	
    45	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    46	
    47	Therefore, the risk minimization problem can be empirically approximated by ˆ θ glyph[defines] arg min θ 1 n ∑ n i =1 l ( z i , h θ ) . For convenience, we denote l ( z i , h θ ) by l i ( θ ) . The main notations are listed in Table 1.
    48	
    49	Weighted subsampling. For a general subsampling framework, each sample is assigned with a random variable O i ∼ Bern ( π i ) , indicating whether this sample is selected or not, such that [ O i = 1] ↔ [ z i is selected]. The weighted subsampling methods have the similar form of objective function R w on the subset:
    50	
    51	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    52	
    53	where each term is assigned the inversion of its sampling probability 1 /π i as the weight. It is similar to the technique
    54	
    55	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    56	
    57	Table 1: Main notation.
    58	
    59	training and testing sample, ∈ R d × R . full-set-model and subset-model, ∈ R d . z i 's influence of whole test set risk, ∈ R . z i 's influence of model parameter, ∈ R d . perturbation put on z i 's loss term, ∈ R . sampling probability of z i , ∈ R + . model θ 's risk on training sample z i , ∈ R . model θ 's risk on test sample z j , ∈ R . training distribution. a specific test distribution.
    60	
    61	used in Causal Inference to handle the selection bias (Schnabel et al. 2016), the Eq.(3) derives the expectation of R w on O :
    62	
    63	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    64	
    65	The expectation of the R w on a subset is the same as the empirical risk on the full set, which means the weighted subsampling methods aim at finding optimal { π i } n i =1 to let the subset risk minimizer ˜ θ as close to the full-set risk minimizer ˆ θ as possible.
    66	
    67	This weighted approach has three main challenges: 1) we need to modify the existing training procedures to accommodate the weighted loss function; 2) because π can be small and its inversion ranges widely, the weighted loss function suffers from high variance; and 3) most importantly, as the weighted methods build a consistent estimator of the full-set-model (Ai et al. 2018), it theoretically assumes that subset-model cannot outperform the full-set-model.
    68	
    69	Unweighted subsampling. We propose a novel Unweighted subsampling method which does not require 1 π i in its objective function:
    70	
    71	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    72	
    73	where the | · | means cardinality of the set. From Eq.(4), we can find the expectation of subset-model's risk is no longer equal to the full-set-model's. This formula can be seen as reweighting the samples with respect to their sampling probabilities implicitly. It directly overcomes the first two challenges in weighted subsampling above-mentioned, but further efforts are required to solve the last one, which will be introduced in the following section. An intuitive demonstration of the difference between the weighted and unweighted methods is shown as Fig. 1.
    74	
    75	Influence functions. If the i ′ th training sample is upweighted by a small glyph[epsilon1] ′ corresponding to its loss term, then the perturbed risk minimizer is
    76	
    77	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    78	
    79	Figure 1: (a) if the blue points (training samples) within the red circle are removed, the new optimal decision boundary is still same as the former one; (b) if removing blue points in the red circle, the new decision boundary shifts from the left, while achieves better performance on the Te set.
    80	
    81	<!-- image -->
    82	
    83	The basic idea of IF is to approximate the change of parameter θ (Cook and Weisberg 1980):
    84	
    85	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    86	
    87	or test risk on a given test distribution Q ′ from (Koh and Liang 2017):
    88	
    89	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
    90	
    91	where we have z i , z j from training and test set, and the H ˆ θ glyph[defines] 1 n ∑ n i =1 ∇ 2 θ l i ( ˆ θ ) is the Hessian matrix based on full set risk minimizer ˆ θ , which is positive definite (PD) if the empirical risk is twice-differentiable and strictly convex in θ .
    92	
    93	## Methodology
    94	
    95	The key challenge lies on that the ˆ θ may not be the best risk minimizer corresponding to Q ′ , due to the distribution shift between P and Q ′ and unknown noisy samples in the training set. With the advent of IF, we can measure one sample's influence on test distribution without prohibitive leave-oneout training. The essential philosophy here is, given a test distribution Q ′ , some samples in training set cause increasing test risk. If they are downweighted, accordingly we can have less test risk than before, namely R ˜ θ ( Q ′ ) ≤ R ˆ θ ( Q ′ ) where the ˜ θ is the new model learned after some harmful samples are downweighted.
    96	
    97	## Subset can be better
    98	
    99	Considering there are n perturbations put on each training sample: glyph[vector] glyph[epsilon1] = ( glyph[epsilon1] 1 , glyph[epsilon1] 2 , ..., glyph[epsilon1] n ) glyph[latticetop] , the perturbed risk minimizer is denoted as ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] . Given m samples { z j } j m =1 from another distribution Q ′ , the objective is to design the glyph[vector] glyph[epsilon1] that minimizes the test risk R ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] ( Q ′ ) = 1 m ∑ m j =1 l j ( ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] ) .
   100	
   101	According to the definition of IF in Eq. (7), we can approximate the loss change of z j ∼ Q ′ if z i ∼ P is upweighed by a glyph[epsilon1] i :
   102	
   103	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   104	
   105	which can be extended to the whole test distribution as following:
   106	
   107	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   108	
   109	For convenience, we use φ i ( ˆ θ ) glyph[defines] ∑ m j =1 φ ( z i , z j , ˆ θ ) to indicate one training sample z i 's influence over whole Q ′ . Therefore, with the ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] under the perturbation of glyph[vector] glyph[epsilon1] , the test risk change can be approximated as following 2 :
   110	
   111	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   112	
   113	Specifically, suppose when π i = 1 for ∀ i = 1 , 2 , ..., n , the subset is the same as the full set. In this situation, the ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] = ˆ θ such that l j ( ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] ) -l j ( θ ) = 0 for ∀ j = 1 , 2 .., m . Based on this analysis, we have the Lemma 1. For clear notion, we use bold letters to represent random variables, such that the φ i ( ˆ θ ) and glyph[epsilon1] i are the realization of the random variables φ and glyph[epsilon1] , respectively.
   114	
   115	Lemma 1. The expectation of the influence function φ i ( ˆ θ ) over training distribution P is always 0, which means:
   116	
   117	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   118	
   119	According to the Eq. (10), minimizing test risk is equivalent to minimizing the objective function 1 n ∑ n i =1 glyph[epsilon1] i φ i ( ˆ θ ) . Actually, this objective function is empirical form of the E P ( φ × glyph[epsilon1] ) from which we derive the Lemma 2.
   120	
   121	Lemma 2. The subset-model ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] performs not worse than the full-set-model ˆ θ in terms of test risk R ( Q ′ ) if glyph[epsilon1] and φ are negative correlated:
   122	
   123	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   124	
   125	The Lemma 2 gives instruction that making the perturbation glyph[epsilon1] negative correlated with IF φ can ensure better subsetmodel ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] . To this end we can let the glyph[epsilon1] ( φ ) a decreasing function. The proof of Lemma 1 and Lemma 2 can be seen in the supplementary appendix A and B, respectively.
   126	
   127	2 We assume that all elements in glyph[vector] glyph[epsilon1] are small, and each training sample z i influences the test risk independently, hence we add up all terms linearly for simplicity of implementation.
   128	
   129	## Deterministic v.s. Probabilistic sampling
   130	
   131	Similar to the Eq.(3), the expectation of φ on subset via the observation variable O can be acquired by:
   132	
   133	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   134	
   135	However, the objective function in Eq. (10) is defined on perturbation glyph[epsilon1] instead of sampling probability π , so that we need to bridge the gap between them:
   136	
   137	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   138	
   139	The Eq. (14) holds because from Lemma 1 we know the E P ( φ ) = 0 . Here we assume that glyph[epsilon1] ∈ [ -1 n , 0] 3 , therefore, if we let π = nglyph[epsilon1] +1 ∈ [0 , 1] , then the perturbation is transformed to sampling probability, because the Eq.(13) and Eq. (14) are in the same form.
   140	
   141	In fact, the Eq.(13) has closed form of optimal π i :
   142	
   143	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   144	
   145	This form of sampling is termed as Data dropout in (Wang, Huan, and Li 2018) while here we call it Deterministic sampling , because it simply sets a threshold and selects samples deterministically. By contrast, sampling with a continuous function π ( φ ) ∈ [0 , 1] is called Probabilistic sampling since each sample has a probability to be selected. Most of sampling studies belong to probabilistic sampling methods: (Wang, Zhu, and Ma 2018) builds π mMSE and π mVc based on A-optimality and L-optimality respectively, and (Ting and Brochu 2018) uses π i ∝ ‖ ψ θ ( z i ) ‖ .
   146	
   147	## Analysis of sampling functions
   148	
   149	For Dropout method, the sample's influence over a Q ′ is the essential criterion for sampling, such that the obtained subset-model ends up being optimal only for a Q ′ . However, the subset-model's robustness to distribution shift (Hu et al. 2018) is also a concern. That is, for a set of distributions around the empirical one, whether our subset-model can still maintain its performance. In this work, we postulate that the Influence-based subsampling confronts to trade the subset-model's performance on a specific Q ′ off its distributional robustness. In this viewpoint, the Dropout method is overly confident on a Q ′ at the expense of deteriorating generalization ability, hence it is reasonable to measure and control this confidence degree for our subsampling methods.
   150	
   151	Considering an uncertainty set Q = { Q | Q glyph[lessmuch] P, D χ 2 ( Q ‖ P ) ≤ δ, δ ≥ 0 } , where the Q glyph[lessmuch] P denotes that Q is absolutely continuous w.r.t. P , and D χ 2 ( ·‖· ) means χ 2 -divergence:
   152	
   153	3 The glyph[epsilon1] = 0 means no perturbation is applied, while the glyph[epsilon1] = -1 n means a sample is totally dropped in objective function, here all perturbations are assumed within this interval.
   154	
   155	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   156	
   157	The Q is a χ 2 -divergence distribution ball, indicating all neighborhoods of the empirical distribution P . The worstcase risk R ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] ( Q ) is defined as the supremum of the risk over any Q ∈ Q (Bagnell 2005):
   158	
   159	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   160	
   161	From the (Duchi and Namkoong 2018), the dual form of the Eq. (17) is:
   162	
   163	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   164	
   165	where the η is the dual variable. This duality transforms the supremum in Eq.(17) to a convex function on the empirical distribution P , thus allowing us to measure the worst-case risk quantitatively. Before focusing on analyzing how the worst-case risk R ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] ( Q ) changes with the different sampling functions in Theorem 3, we need to introduce two terms:
   166	
   167	Definition 1. A function f(x): R d → R is said to be Lipschitz continuous with constant ξ if ‖∇ f ( x ) - ∇ f ( y ) ‖ ≤ ξ ‖ x -y ‖ , ∀ x, y ∈ R d .
   168	
   169	Definition 2. A function f(x) has σ -bounded gradients if ‖∇ f ( x ) ‖ ≤ σ for all x ∈ R d .
   170	
   171	Theorem 3. Let η ∗ the optimal dual variable η that achieves the infimum in the Eq. (4) , and the perturbation function glyph[epsilon1] ( φ ) has σ -bounded gradients. Then, the worst-case risk R ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] ( η ∗ ) is a Lipschitz continuos function w.r.t. the IF vector glyph[vector] φ = ( φ 1 , φ 2 , .., φ n ) glyph[latticetop] where we have the Lipschitz constant ξ = O ( σ √ 2 δ +1 n ) , that is
   172	
   173	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   174	
   175	The Theorem 3 relate the change rate of the worst-case risk to the gradient bound σ of the perturbation function glyph[epsilon1] ( φ ) . For the Dropout method, its sampling function Eq.(15) has unbounded gradient since it is inconsistent at the zero point, causing the σ → ∞ . This property makes R ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] ( η ∗ ) no longer Lipschitz continuous and suffers sharp fluctuation. By contrast, our probabilistic methods can adjust the confidence degree by tuning the σ . This is crucial to avoid over confidence on a specific Q ′ that leads to large risk on other Q ∈ Q . In fact, our experiments bear out that our probabilistic methods maintain its performance out-of-sample with proper σ , while the Dropout method often crashes. The proof of Theorem 3 can be found in Appendix C.
   176	
   177	## Surrogate metric for confidence degree
   178	
   179	Nevertheless, we find that σ is the determinant of confidence degree, it is still intractable to measure this degree quantitatively, which is important to guide our methods use in practice. Empirically, to deal with over fitting, practitioners prefer adding constraints on the model's parameters θ , e.g. l 2 -norm regularizer. In our theory, we propose to apply the Γ( glyph[vector] φ ) = ‖ ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] -ˆ θ ‖ 2 to evaluate the confidence degree over a specific Q ′ . We term the Γ( glyph[vector] φ ) a surrogate metric for confidence degree, and prove in Theorem 4 that it is reasonable because the Γ( glyph[vector] φ ) has the same magnitude of Lipschitz constant as the R ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] ( η ∗ ) . In detail, the worst-case risk and our surrogate metric share the same change rate corresponding to the sampling function's gradient bound σ .
   180	
   181	Theorem 4. Let the perturbation function glyph[epsilon1] ( φ ) has σ -bounded gradient, and the | glyph[epsilon1] ( φ ) | is bounded by τ ∈ R + , that is | glyph[epsilon1] ( φ ) | ≤ τ . We have the parameter shift Γ( glyph[vector] φ ) = ‖ ˆ θ glyph[epsilon1] -ˆ θ ‖ 2 is Lischitz continuous with its Lipschitz constant ξ = O ( στ ) . Specifically for τ = 1 n , we have ξ = O ( σ n ) .
   182	
   183	The Theorem 4 is helpful to measure our sampling methods confidence degree in practice if the radius δ of the χ 2 -divergence ball is unknown. Theoretically, a relatively small σ guarantees more robust model. The proof of the Theorem 4 can be found in the supplementary appendix D.
   184	
   185	Figure 2: Our unweighted subsampling framework.
   186	
   187	<!-- image -->
   188	
   189	## Implementation
   190	
   191	In this section, the unweighted subsampling method is incorprated in our framework, shown in Fig.2: we train ˆ θ with the full set data, and calculate the IF vector glyph[vector] φ = ( φ 1 , φ 2 , ..., φ n ) on training set with the ˆ θ . The sampling probabilities are acquired with the designed probabilistic sampling function π ( · ) afterwards. We will discuss the two basic modules of this framework: 1) calculating the IF and 2) designing probabilistic sampling functions.
   192	
   193	## Calculating influence functions
   194	
   195	The IF in Eq.(7) can be calculated in two steps: first calculating the inverse Hessian-vector-product (HVP) ∇ θ l j ( ˆ θ ) glyph[latticetop] H -1 ˆ θ , then multiply it with the ∇ θ l i ( ˆ θ ) for each training sample. To handle the sparse scenarios when the H ˆ θ has high dimensions, (Martens 2010) proposes to transform the inverse HVP into an optimization problem: H -1 ˆ θ v ≡ arg min t { 1 2 t glyph[latticetop] H ˆ θ t -v glyph[latticetop] t } , and solve it with Newton conjugate gradient (Newton-CG) method. Moreover, the (Agar- wal, Bullins, and Hazan 2017) proves that the stochastic estimation makes the calculation feasible when the loss function l ( · ) is non-convex in θ . These works ensure our framework's feasibility in both convex and non-convex scenarios. Without loss of generality, we mainly focus on convex scenarios.
   196	
   197	Figure 3: A family of sampling functions, the x axis is value of influence function φ and the y axis is the probability π .
   198	
   199	<!-- image -->
   200	
   201	When the CG converges slowly because of ill-conditioned sub-problem, the mixed preconditioner ¯ M is useful to reduce CG steps(Nash 1985; Hsia, Chiang, and Lin 2018):
   202	
   203	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   204	
   205	where the α is a weight parameter, I is the identical matrix and H θ is the Hessian matrix. Specifically for logistic regression model, its diagonal elements on H θ is:
   206	
   207	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   208	
   209	where the C is the regularization parameter. Our experiments demonstrate that the Mixed PCG is efficacious for speeding up the calculation of IF.
   210	
   211	## Probabilistic sampling functions
   212	
   213	From Lemma 2, better subset-model is ensured with a decreasing function π ( φ ) . Furthermore, the Theorem 3 and 4 prove that the gradient bound σ of π ( φ ) can be adjusted for confidence degree over a Q ′ . We can design a family of probabilistic sampling functions with tunable hyper parameter w.r.t. σ . Here we develop two basic functions, termed as Linear sampling and Sigmoid sampling .
   214	
   215	Linear sampling. Inspired by (Ting and Brochu 2018), which builds π i ∝ ‖ ψ θ ( z i ) ‖ as π i = max { α, min { 1 , λ ‖ ψ θ ( z i ) ‖}} , we design a Linear sampling function where we let π i ∝ -φ i ( ˆ θ ) :
   216	
   217	<!-- formula-not-decoded -->
   218	
   219	where α ∈ R + . It is easy to prove the gradient bound σ of π ( φ ) is α , thus the degree of confidence relies on the α for the Linear sampling. It is a little different from the π i ∝ ‖ ψ θ ( z i ) ‖ , because the φ i can be both negative and positive, which means many samples can have zero probability to be sampled. If we set a relatively high sampling ratio, like 90% or higher, we will never get enough samples in our subset. Empirically, we find randomly picking up the negative π samples reaches relatively good results.

   225	Sigmoid sampling. The Sigmoid function is generally used in logistic regression to scale the outputs into [0 , 1] , which indicates probability of each class, such that here we can use it to transform φ i to probability as following:
   226

   228	
   229	where α ∈ R + . For the Sigmoid function, we can still adjust the α to make the probability distribution more flat or steep, thereby control the confidence degree.
   230	
   231	## Experiments
   232	
   233	In this section, we present data sets and experiment settings at first, and introduce several baselines for comparison. After that, we do experiments to evaluate our methods in terms of effectiveness, robustness and efficiency.
   234	
   235	## Data sets
   236	
   237	We perform extensive experiments on various public data sets which conclude many domains, including computer vision, natural language processing, click-through rate prediction, etc. Additionally, we test the methods on the Company data set which contains around 100 million samples with over 10 million features. They are queries collected from a real world recommender system, whose feature set contains user history behaviour, item's side information and contextual information, such as time and location. These data sets range from small to large, from low dimensions to high dimensions, which can testify the methods effectiveness and robustness in diverse scenarios. The data set statistics and more details about preprocessing on some data sets are described in appendix E.
   238	
   239	## Considered baselines
   240	
   241	We select the model trained on full data set and other three sampling approaches as the baselines for comparison: Optimal sampling (Ting and Brochu 2018), Data dropout (Wang, Huan, and Li 2018) and Random sampling.
   242	
   243	- A. Optimal sampling. It is a recent research in weighted subsampling that uses sampling probability proportional to ‖ ψ θ ‖ : π i = max { α, min { 1 , λ ‖ ψ θ ( z i ) ‖}} . This method aims at best approaching full set performance such that it cannot overtake full set theoretically.
   244	- B. Data dropout. It is an unweighted subsampling approach which adopts a simple sampling strategy that is dropping out unfavorable samples whose φ i > 0 .
   245	- C. Random sampling. It is simple random selection on Tr which means all samples share same probability. Theoretically, this strategy cannot win over full set as well.
   246	
   247	Figure 5: Test ACC with noisy labels (40% being flipped).
   248	
   249	<!-- image -->
   250	
   251	## Experiment protocols
   252	
   253	In our experiments, we use a Tr-Va-Te setting which is different from the Tr-Va setting as many previous work do (see the Fig. 4). Both settings proceed in three steps, and share the same first two steps: 1) training model ˆ θ on the full Tr, predicting on the Va, then computing the IF; 2) getting sampling probability from the IF, doing sampling on Tr to get the subset, then acquiring the subset-model ˜ θ . In the third step, we introduce an additional out-of-sample test set (Te) to test the ˜ θ (step (b.3)) rather than testing the ˜ θ on the Va (step (a.3)). The reason is that if we use the ˆ θ 's validation loss on the Va to guide the subsampling and then train the subsetmodel ˜ θ , the testing result of ˜ θ on the Va cannot convince us of its generalization ability.
   254	
   255	In fact, our framework is applicable for both convex and non-convex models, and we mainly focus on subsampling theory in this work. For implementation simplicity, we use logistic regression in all experiments. Besides, to ensure that our methods indeed achieve good performance in terms of the metrics they optimized for, i.e., we use the logistic loss for computing the influence function, such that the logloss is used as the metric in all experiments. More details about experimental settings can be found in appendix F.
   256	
   257	Figure 6: Comparison on logloss between Va set and Te set.
   258	
   259	<!-- image -->
   260	
   261	## Experiment observations
   262	
   263	Result 1: Effectiveness. The experimental results are shown in the Table 2 where sampling ratio is set as 95% for all sampling methods. The average test logloss 4 values are listed in each column. The bold letters indicate logloss less than the full-set-model, and the underlying ones are the best across the row. It can be seen that our Sig- and LinUIDS overhaul the full-set-model on most of data sets, while Dropout often fails. Besides, due to the high variance incurred by weight term, the OptLR method severely suffers. In a nutshell, the Sig-UIDS performs the best on 5 of 14 datasets, and both Lin-UIDS and Dropout achieves 4. That means over-confidence sometimes is beneficial on those homogeneous data sets, e.g. the MNIST, but the Dropout fails on all relatively large-scale and heterogeneous data sets. The probabilistic sampling methods have universal superiority over others, since it keeps robustness on a set of distributions Q , rather than a specific Q ′ (the Va set).

   312	
   313	## Conclusion & Future Work
   314	
   315	In this work, we theoretically study the unweighted subsampling with IF, then propose a novel unweighted subsampling framework and design a family of probabilistic sampling methods. The experiments show that 1) different from the previous weighted methods, our unweighted method can acquire the subset-model that indeed wins over the full-setmodel on a given test set; 2) it is crucial to evaluate the con- fidence degree over the empirical distribution for enhancing our subset-model ˜ θ 's generalization ability.
   316	
   317	Although our framework of the Unweighted Influence Data Subsampling (UIDS) method succeeds in improving model accuracy, there are still some interesting ideas remain to be explored. Since our framework is applicable both for convex and non-convex models, we can further testify its performance on those non-convex models, e.g. Deep Neural Networks. Another direction is to develop better approaches to deal with the over fitting issue, e.g. build a validation set selection scheme. Besides, we plan to implement our method in industry in the future.

   469	## Appendix E. Data Sets and Experimental Settings
   470	
   471	## Data set
   472	
   473	The data sets statistics can be found in Table 1, and several of them are processed specifically.
   474	
   475	MNIST, cifar10 and SVHN. They are all 10-classes image classification data sets while Logistic regression can only handle binary classification. On MNIST and SVHN, we select the number 1 and 7 as positive and negative classes; On cifar10, we do classification on cat and dog. For each image we convert all pixels to flattened feature values with all being scaled by 1 / 255 .
   476	
   477	Covertype. It is a multi-class forest cover type classification dataset which is transformed to binary class and all features are scaled to [0 , 1] .
   478	
   479	News20. This is a size-balanced two-class variant of the UCI 20 Newsgroup data set where the each class contains 10 classes and each example vector is normalized to unit length.
   480	
   481	Criteo1%. It is used in a CTR prediction competition held jointly by Kaggle and Criteo in 2014. The data used here is conducted feature engineering according to winning solution in this competition. We ramdomly sample 1% examples from the original data set.
   482	
   483	Avazu-app and Avazu-site. This data is used in a CTR prediction competition held jointly by Kaggle and Avazu in 2014. Here the data is generated according to winning solution where the data is split into two groups 'app' and 'site' for better performance.
   484	
   485	## Experimental settings
   486	
   487	For logistic regression on both full set and subset, we select the regularization term C = 0 . 1 for fair comparison. For the Optimal sampling methods, we set λ = 1 / max {‖ ψ θ ( z i ) ‖} n i =1 to scale the probability into [0 , 1] and set α = 0 . 01 to prevent the 1 π from large variance following. For Data dropout method, we rank the samples by their IF and select the top ones; For Linear sampling function, we set α = 1 / max {| φ i |} n i =1 similar to the Optimal sampling and we randomly pick those unfavorable samples if samples with φ < 0 are not enough to reach the objective sampling ratio; for Sigmoid sampling, we set α ∈ { 0 . 1 , 1 , 5 , 10 , 50 } .
   488	
   489	For public data, we randomly pick up 30% data from Tr as the Va for each data set. For the company data, with domain knowledge we use 7 days data as Tr, 1 day for Va and 1 day for Te. For all subsampling methods, the Tr, Va and Te maintain the same for fair comparison. Besides, to make the test logloss comparable among different subsampling methods, postive-negative sample ratio is kept invariant after subsampling for all methods, which avoids the test logloss being influenced by the shift of label ratio.

1:## Less Is Better: Unweighted Data Subsampling via Influence Function
11:In the time of Big Data , training complex models on largescale data sets is challenging, making it appealing to reduce data volume for saving computation resources by subsampling. Most previous works in subsampling are weighted methods designed to help the performance of subset-model approach the full-set-model, hence the weighted methods have no chance to acquire a subset-model that is better than the full-set-model. However, we question that how can we achieve better model with less data? In this work, we propose a novel Unweighted Influence Data Subsampling (UIDS) method, and prove that the subset-model acquired through our method can outperform the full-set-model. Besides, we show that overly confident on a given test set for sampling is common in Influence-based subsampling methods, which can eventually cause our subset-model's failure in out-ofsample test. To mitigate it, we develop a probabilistic sampling scheme to control the worst-case risk over all distributions close to the empirical distribution. The experiment results demonstrate our methods superiority over existed subsampling methods in diverse tasks, such as text classification, image classification, click-through prediction, etc.
69:Unweighted subsampling. We propose a novel Unweighted subsampling method which does not require 1 π i in its objective function:
317:Although our framework of the Unweighted Influence Data Subsampling (UIDS) method succeeds in improving model accuracy, there are still some interesting ideas remain to be explored. Since our framework is applicable both for convex and non-convex models, we can further testify its performance on those non-convex models, e.g. Deep Neural Networks. Another direction is to develop better approaches to deal with the over fitting issue, e.g. build a validation set selection scheme. Besides, we plan to implement our method in industry in the future.

name: Grep
content:
11:In the time of Big Data , training complex models on largescale data sets is challenging, making it appealing to reduce data volume for saving computation resources by subsampling. Most previous works in subsampling are weighted methods designed to help the performance of subset-model approach the full-set-model, hence the weighted methods have no chance to acquire a subset-model that is better than the full-set-model. However, we question that how can we achieve better model with less data? In this work, we propose a novel Unweighted Influence Data Subsampling (UIDS) method, and prove that the subset-model acquired through our method can outperform the full-set-model. Besides, we show that overly confident on a given test set for sampling is common in Influence-based subsampling methods, which can eventually cause our subset-model's failure in out-ofsample test. To mitigate it, we develop a probabilistic sampling scheme to control the worst-case risk over all distributions close to the empirical distribution. The experiment results demonstrate our methods superiority over existed subsampling methods in diverse tasks, such as text classification, image classification, click-through prediction, etc.
27:Our work can be outlined in four points. First, instead of approaching the full-set-model ˆ θ , we prove that the model ˜ θ trained by a selected subset through our subsampling method can outperform the ˆ θ ; second, we propose several probabilistic sampling functions, and analyze how the sampling function influences the worst-case risk (Bagnell 2005) changes over a χ 2 -divergence ball. We further propose a surrogate metric to measure the confidence degree of the sampling methods over the observed distribution, which is useful for evaluating model's generalization ability on a set of distributions; third, for the sake of implementation efficiency, the Hessian-free mixed Preconditioned Conjugate Gradient (PCG) method is used to compute the influence function (IF) in sparse scenarios; last, complete experiments are conducted on diverse tasks to demonstrate our methods superiority over the existing state-of-the-art subsampling methods 1 .
145:This form of sampling is termed as Data dropout in (Wang, Huan, and Li 2018) while here we call it Deterministic sampling , because it simply sets a threshold and selects samples deterministically. By contrast, sampling with a continuous function π ( φ ) ∈ [0 , 1] is called Probabilistic sampling since each sample has a probability to be selected. Most of sampling studies belong to probabilistic sampling methods: (Wang, Zhu, and Ma 2018) builds π mMSE and π mVc based on A-optimality and L-optimality respectively, and (Ting and Brochu 2018) uses π i ∝ ‖ ψ θ ( z i ) ‖ .
191:In this section, the unweighted subsampling method is incorprated in our framework, shown in Fig.2: we train ˆ θ with the full set data, and calculate the IF vector glyph[vector] φ = ( φ 1 , φ 2 , ..., φ n ) on training set with the ˆ θ . The sampling probabilities are acquired with the designed probabilistic sampling function π ( · ) afterwards. We will discuss the two basic modules of this framework: 1) calculating the IF and 2) designing probabilistic sampling functions.
213:From Lemma 2, better subset-model is ensured with a decreasing function π ( φ ) . Furthermore, the Theorem 3 and 4 prove that the gradient bound σ of π ( φ ) can be adjusted for confidence degree over a Q ′ . We can design a family of probabilistic sampling functions with tunable hyper parameter w.r.t. σ . Here we develop two basic functions, termed as Linear sampling and Sigmoid sampling .
263:Result 1: Effectiveness. The experimental results are shown in the Table 2 where sampling ratio is set as 95% for all sampling methods. The average test logloss 4 values are listed in each column. The bold letters indicate logloss less than the full-set-model, and the underlying ones are the best across the row. It can be seen that our Sig- and LinUIDS overhaul the full-set-model on most of data sets, while Dropout often fails. Besides, due to the high variance incurred by weight term, the OptLR method severely suffers. In a nutshell, the Sig-UIDS performs the best on 5 of 14 datasets, and both Lin-UIDS and Dropout achieves 4. That means over-confidence sometimes is beneficial on those homogeneous data sets, e.g. the MNIST, but the Dropout fails on all relatively large-scale and heterogeneous data sets. The probabilistic sampling methods have universal superiority over others, since it keeps robustness on a set of distributions Q , rather than a specific Q ′ (the Va set).
315:In this work, we theoretically study the unweighted subsampling with IF, then propose a novel unweighted subsampling framework and design a family of probabilistic sampling methods. The experiments show that 1) different from the previous weighted methods, our unweighted method can acquire the subset-model that indeed wins over the full-setmodel on a given test set; 2) it is crucial to evaluate the con- fidence degree over the empirical distribution for enhancing our subset-model ˜ θ 's generalization ability.
